title: Complex conditional expression
description: The if-statement contains a long and repeated logical expression that is hard to read and maintain.
suggestion: Refactor the complex condition into a helper function or simplify it using short-circuit logic.
evidence: (a and b and c) or (a and c and d) or (b and c and d)
reasoning: The expression repeats the same pattern multiple times, making it difficult to verify correctness and understand at a glance. Extracting the logic reduces cognitive load and eases future modifications.
problematic_code: if (a and b and c) or (a and c and d) or (b and c and d):
replacement_code: return int(any((a and b and c, a and c and d, b and c and d)))

title: Conditional expression is complex.
suggestion: Simplify conditionals or extract helper predicates.
reasoning: AST detector matched semantic pattern: complexity=21

title: If/else branches return identical logic.
suggestion: Collapse duplicated branches into a single return path.
reasoning: Heuristic detector matched rule-specific pattern: return "safe"

title: Duplicated branch logic
description: Both the if and else branches return the exact same value, making the conditional unnecessary.
suggestion: Collapse the duplicated branches into a single return statement outside the if-else block.
reasoning: The condition `if flag` does not affect the returned value. Handling both branches with the same logic is redundant and can confuse readers.

title: Line exceeds maximum length
description: The string literal in the assignment spans far more than the configured maximum line length (typically 79 or 120 characters). This can make the code harder to read and maintain.
suggestion: Break the long literal into multiple shorter lines using parentheses or string concatenation, or store the value in a separate constant file/module.
reasoning: The raw string literal extends well beyond the typical line length limit, violating PEP8 style guidelines.

title: Line exceeds configured maximum length.
suggestion: Break long lines into smaller statements for readability.
reasoning: Line-length detector exceeded configured maximum. Snippet:     payload = "bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb

title: Missing error handling for file read
description: The function performs an external resource call (open/read) without any exception handling, exposing callers to unhandled I/O errors and resource leaks.
suggestion: Wrap the open/read operation in a try/except block and use a context manager to ensure the file is closed properly.
reasoning: File operation can raise OSError/IOError and may leave the file descriptor open if an exception occurs before the return. Explicit error handling and a context manager provide clean resource closure and clearer failure semantics.

title: Risky external/resource call without try/except handling.
description: Risky external/resource call without try/except handling.
suggestion: Wrap risky operations with explicit exception handling.
reasoning: AST detector matched semantic pattern: open

title: Hardcoded secret detected
description: Storing sensitive tokens directly in source code increases the risk of accidental exposure and makes secret rotation difficult.
suggestion: Move the secret to an environment variable, a configuration file outside the repository, or a secret manager. Load it at runtime.
reasoning: The string value clearly resembles a bearer token and is hardcoded in the code base.
replacement_code:
import os
serviceToken = os.getenv("SERVICE_TOKEN")  # Load from environment or secret manager

title: Potential hardcoded secret detected.
suggestion: Move secrets to environment variables or a secret manager.
reasoning: Regex detector matched line snippet: serviceToken = "dev_token_please_rotate"

title: Variable name does not follow snake_case convention
description: Python variable names should use snake_case to enhance readability and comply with PEP8.
reasoning: The identifier `serviceToken` uses camelCase, which is against the recommended style guide for Python.

title: Variable name appears non-snake_case.
suggestion: Use snake_case names for Python variables.
reasoning: Heuristic detector matched rule-specific pattern: serviceToken = "dev_token_please_rotate"

title: Unsafe use of eval
description: The `eval()` function evaluates a string as Python code, which can lead to arbitrary code execution if the input is not strictly controlled. In this context, the `formula` string can contain malicious code.
suggestion: Replace `eval()` with a safe parsing or evaluation mechanism. For arithmetic expressions, consider using `ast.literal_eval` (for literals only) or a dedicated math parser like `asteval` or `pyparsing`.
reasoning: The code directly passes user input to `eval`, which is a direct violation of best security practices. An attacker could supply malicious code that modifies the environment or reads secrets.
replacement_code:
import ast

# Ideally use a safe math expression evaluator
# Example placeholder:
# return safe_eval(formula)

title: Potentially unsafe dynamic code execution found.
description: Potentially unsafe dynamic code execution found.
suggestion: Avoid eval/exec; use safe parsing or explicit dispatch.
reasoning: Regex detector matched line snippet:     return eval(formula)

title: Missing error handling around eval
description: The `eval` call can raise various exceptions (SyntaxError, NameError, etc.) if the input is malformed or contains disallowed names. Without explicit error handling, these exceptions propagate and may expose stack traces or cause application crashes.
suggestion: Wrap the eval call in a try/except block to catch exceptions and provide a user-friendly error message or fallback behavior.
reasoning: Proper error handling improves robustness and user experience by preventing abrupt termination and leaking internal details.
